coredata.dc:title: A simple model of bubble cluster dynamics in an acoustic field
coredata.dc:creator: Fan, Yuzhe
Li, Haisen
Zhu, Jianjun
Du, Weidong
coredata.dc:description:
The dynamics of bubble clouds induced by ultrasound field are investigated in a regime where the cloud size is much smaller than the ultrasound wavelength. Two frequently used models describing the dynamics of individual bubbles inside a bubble cluster in an acoustic field are studied, one based on the homogeneity assumption, and the other based on the simultaneous motion assumption. A modified formula of the homogenization-based model is presented, and an inherent distinction in bubble-bubble interaction term is found in comparison to the simultaneous motion model. To gain insight into the mechanisms of such distinction, a reduced model unifying these two models is presented, and such distinction is explained by the spatial dependence of the bubble-bubble interaction in a bubble cluster accordingly. To validate the reduced model, the normalized distance
γ
bb
and the cloud interaction parameter
B
0
are used as two scaling parameters, and the comparison between the present model and the coupled Rayleigh-Plesset type equations is made. A conclusion is that, in the weak bubble-bubble interaction case (
γ
bb
> 10
), the reduced model can well reproduce the radial motion of bubbles in the cluster during the growth stage and the collapse stage in each acoustic cycle; in the strong bubble-bubble interaction case (
γ
bb
< 10
), the growth phase of bubbles in the cluster can be accurately predicted by the reduced model only if
B
0
or the amplitude of driving field is small.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cavitation
Micro bubble
Bubble cluster dynamics

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Project No. 41576102, and No. U1709203 were supported by the NSFC. Project No. 3072019GIP0506 was supported by Ph.D. Student Research and Innovation Fund of the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities .

head.ce:title: A simple model of bubble cluster dynamics in an acoustic field

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Underwater Acoustic Engineering, Harbin Engineering University, Harbin 150001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Underwater Acoustic Engineering, Harbin Engineering University, Harbin 150001, China

Ministry of Industry and Information Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Underwater Acoustic Engineering, Harbin Engineering University, Harbin 150001, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The dynamics of bubble clouds induced by ultrasound field are investigated in a regime where the cloud size is much smaller than the ultrasound wavelength. Two frequently used models describing the dynamics of individual bubbles inside a bubble cluster in an acoustic field are studied, one based on the homogeneity assumption, and the other based on the simultaneous motion assumption. A modified formula of the homogenization-based model is presented, and an inherent distinction in bubble-bubble interaction term is found in comparison to the simultaneous motion model. To gain insight into the mechanisms of such distinction, a reduced model unifying these two models is presented, and such distinction is explained by the spatial dependence of the bubble-bubble interaction in a bubble cluster accordingly. To validate the reduced model, the normalized distance
γ
bb
and the cloud interaction parameter
B
0
are used as two scaling parameters, and the comparison between the present model and the coupled Rayleigh-Plesset type equations is made. A conclusion is that, in the weak bubble-bubble interaction case (
γ
bb
> 10
), the reduced model can well reproduce the radial motion of bubbles in the cluster during the growth stage and the collapse stage in each acoustic cycle; in the strong bubble-bubble interaction case (
γ
bb
< 10
), the growth phase of bubbles in the cluster can be accurately predicted by the reduced model only if
B
0
or the amplitude of driving field is small.

ce:section.ce:para: Cavitation is the violent motion of a number of individual gas bubbles. Up to the present, the dynamics of an isolated bubble in an acoustic field, which can be described with the Rayleigh-Plesset (R-P) type equations [1–3], has made a progress. However, bubbles often occur in ensembles or clusters, and the interactions between bubbles in bubble clusters are very complex. In fact, it is the dynamics of bubble clusters that determine the details of many phenomena, such as sonoluminescence, sonochemistry, ultrasound contrast, ultrasonic cleaner, lithotripsy and high-intensity focused ultrasound [4–10], etc.

The modeling of a single bubble cluster is challenging and has been the object of several studies [11,12]. Due to the involvement of the bubble-flow interaction, inter-bubble effects and interface capturing, the direct simulation of the bubble cluster dynamics [13–15] is computational cost, and these methods are mostly limited to small scale problems. To study the nonlinear dynamics of spherical bubbles, two phase flow models are more frequently used. Wang & Brennen [16] extended the study of d’Agostino & Brennen [17] to the nonlinear regime, further characterizing the dramatic collapse of bubbles in a single cluster. To study the effect of the polydispersity of nuclei on the nonlinear bubble dynamics in spherical bubble clouds, the similar equations are solved with different numerical approach by Shimada et al. [18]. To capture the evolution of bubbles more accurately, the Eulerian-Lagrangian approach can be used to solve the mixture-averaged equations [19–21,8].

For practical applications, a reduced model, simple enough to be numerically tractable, but properly accounting for the evolution of bubbles in a cluster, is necessary [22–26]. If the number of bubbles in a cluster is small enough, simulations of bubble cloud based on particle methods can be used [27,28]. In these approaches, each bubble is represented by a Lagrangian particle. The radial bubble dynamics is described by a set of R-P type equations which include direct interactions between bubbles [29]. For a larger cluster, due to the significant amount of computational time involved in solving the set of governing nonlinear ordinary differential equations (ODEs), the tracing of the dynamics of every single bubble in the bubble cluster is impeded [30].

In order to further simplify the problem, the mixture of liquid and gas bubbles in the cluster is treated as a continuum medium, whose behavior is described with a homogenization-based model. If we assume that both the radial motion of the cluster boundary and the bubble interfaces can be described by the R-P type equations, the nonlinear dynamics of bubbles in the cluster can be described [31–33]. In these works, the interactions between bubbles are taken into account by the influence of the radial dynamics of the cluster boundary on the bubble dynamics. Alternatively, if we assume that the driving acoustic pressure is homogeneous and the bubbles in the cluster vibrate with an approximately same manner, the microscopic motion of bubbles in the cluster can be described using simultaneous motion model [34,35,23]. In this approach, the set of governing ODEs can be reduced to a single ODE.

However, based on similar assumptions, both the formulations and the bubble dynamics described by above-mentioned two models are inconsistent. The motivation of this work is the derivation of a unifying reduced model, and find the application condition of such reduced model. Using the modified formulation of homogenization-based model, the inherent difference between two models is found. To explain such difference, a reduced model unifying these two models is presented. Using the normalized distance
γ
bb
and the cloud interaction parameter
B
0
, the application condition of the present model is qualitatively given.

The dynamics of an isolated bubble can be studied using the modified Keller-Miksis (K-M) equation [1,2], a variant of the R-P equation, which describes the radial motion of a spherical bubble in a weakly compressible liquid:
(1)

where
a = a
t
is the instantaneous radius of the bubble;
p
in
is the liquid pressure at the interface of the bubble;
p
∞
is the liquid pressure in an infinite domain; c is the speed of sound in the liquid. The mass and thermal exchange is not taken into account and the gas inside bubbles is described by the simple polytropic law (the models discussed in this article can be directly extended to the model containing these phenomena [36,37]). Therefore,
p

-
p
∞

, where
p

is the static pressure;
a
0
is the bubble radius at equilibrium;
σ
is the surface tension;
μ
is the liquid viscosity;
p
t
is the external sound field.

In multibubble cases, if the center-to-center distances between bubbles are small enough, the pressure waves radiated by neighboring bubbles will be nonnegligible. In such a situation, the bubble dynamics can be described by the coupled R-P type model. Suppose that: (1) the radius of the spherical cluster is small in comparison with the wavelength of the external driving field; (2) the mean bubble spacing is large compared to the mean bubble radius (the bubbles are spherical); (3) bubbles in the cluster are spatially homogeneous distributed; (4) slip velocity between the two phases, induced by different external forces [38], is zero. Therefore, only the dynamic properties of bubbles are changed by mutual interactions among the bubbles.

Consider an ensemble of Nspherical bubbles. The pressure wave emitted by bubble ican be obtained from the continuity and Euler equations of fluid flow under the incompressible liquid assumption:

p

Using a system of
2 N
first-order ODEs [30], the coupled R-P type model (Eqs. (3) and (5)) can be solved numerically, and the instantaneous bubble radii and wall velocities of every single bubble in the cluster can be solved accordingly. The coupled R-P type model is used as reference in this article.

For denser cluster, the simultaneous motion model which can reduce the number of the ODEs is frequently used. We assume that locally all bubbles may pulsate in the same manner. Then the number of ODEs to be solved is only 1, and
a
i
in Eq. (5) can be replaced by
a

. In this case, Eq. (5) can be rewritten as:

R-P type equations are derived from the exact hydrodynamic equations in a liquid phase where the pressure is imposed at the border of a spherical region that can contain either a pure gas or a liquid/gas mixture. If we treat the mixture of liquid and gas bubbles in the cluster as a continuum medium, and the pressure inside the cluster is uniform, the radial motion of the cluster boundary can also be described by the R-P type equations. Consider a monodisperse cluster, the mathematical equations of the homogenization-based method is expressed as
(9)

where
R = R
t
is the radius of the cluster;
a = a
t
is the radius of the bubble;
p
c
=
p
c
t
is the liquid pressure in the cluster. In this article, the dynamics of bubbles in the cluster is studied with the K-M equation, and the radial motion of the cluster is described using the R-P equation because of two complementary reasons: (1) the bubbly liquid in the cluster is dilute so that the cluster wall Mach number should be low; (2) the R-P equation is derived under the incompressible liquid assumption which is consistent with the assumption used in Eq. (2).

Eqs. (9) and (10) are complemented with the equation of conservation of liquid volume in the cluster:

Notice that the inherent difference between Eqs. (13) and (6) is the constant coefficient of S.

In this section, we try to find a reasonable explanation of such a difference and clarify some ambiguities. Consider N bubbles randomly distributed in the bubble cluster. Their positions can be given by a multivariate probability density function (PDF)
f

are the conditional probability density function (CPDF). In general,
f
x
i
|
x
j
depends on two aspects: (1) the bubbles vibrate spherically; (2) the forces act on bubbles. For the bubbles pulsating in the same manner, only the second Bjerknes force have contribution to the CPDF (the effect of the other forces (e.g, drag force, etc) on each bubble is the same). In general, the spherical oscillations of bubbles in the cluster only exist steadily in few acoustic cycles, and the influence of the second Bjerknes force on CPDF can thus be ignored. Consistent to the assumption that bubbles vibrate spherically in first few cycles, only a fixed distance
D
min
is taken into account, and there are no pairs of bubbles that can be closer than the distance
D
min
. The CPDF can be written as:
(15)

. It is clear that, for spherical oscillations of bubbles,

(18)

If we substitute
r
0
= 0
and
D
min
= 0
into Eq. (18), Eq. (18) will reduce to the coupling strength of simultaneous motion model; if we substitute
r
0
=
R
0
and
D
min
= 0
into Eq. (18), Eq. (18) will reduce to the coupling strength of homogenization-based model. If we ignore the corrections of bubble distribution, Eq. (18) will recover the formulation proposed by An [39], which an approximate solution of the coupling strength including the effect of weak compressibility.

Since all bubbles are homogeneously distributed in the cluster, from a statistical point of view, the dynamics of the cluster boundary can be described by the R-P type equations. Besides, it is interesting to note that, under the assumption of simultaneous motion, the external pressure of each bubble in the cluster should be the same. For the homogenization-based model, the pressure inside the cluster
p
c
=
p
c
t
is equal to the pressure at the cluster boundary; for the present model, the liquid pressure inside the cluster
p
c
=
p
c
r
0
, t
depends on radial coordinate and time. It is apparent that, for the simultaneous motion model, such pressure is equal to the pressure at the cluster center.

The numerical simulation is performed in the traveling acoustic wave with a driving frequency of 20 kHz in water. The parameters are chosen to keep assumptions valid and set as follows:
ρ = 998
kg/m3, viscosity
μ = 1.002 × 10
−3 kg/ms, surface tension
σ = 0.0725
N/m, polytropic exponent
κ = 1.4
, sound speed c = 1500 m/s, and static pressure p0 = 1 bar. Restricted by the assumption that the radius of the spherical cluster is small in comparison with the wavelength of the external driving field, in general, the reduced models are suitable to low frequency situation (the frequency of the incident wave is much smaller than the nature frequency of the bubble). Therefore, only 20 kHz is used in all the following simulations.

The influence of radial bubble positions
r
0
on bubble dynamics is given in Fig. 1 . (As discussed in Section 2.4, such a comparison also shows the differences between all these models.) Fig. 1 shows that (1) The expansion amplitude of the isolated bubble is noticeably bigger than those of the bubble in the cluster; (2) the isolated bubble reaches its minimum size earlier than the bubble in the cluster. Besides, the peripheral bubble grows to a larger maximum radius and collapse faster than the other bubbles. These results qualitatively reproduces the experimental observations [34,27]. In addition, as shown in Fig. 1, the
D
min
also influence the results. The
D
min
plays an important role in physically closing the reduced model, as well as the coupled R-P type model. Ignoring
D
min
implies that the spherically oscillating bubbles are permitted to overlap.

The Markov chain Monte Carlo method is used to simulate the spatial distribution of the bubbles(Such a method is a iterative procedure. The idea is to construct a Markov chain the states of which are bubble configurations in the cluster. One can find detailed explanation in the Chapter 3.6 of Ref. [40]), and the expansion and collapse of a cluster of bubbles is shown in Fig. 2 . The amplitude of periodic driving field
P
df
is adopted as
1.8
bar, and the initial cluster radius is
R
0
= 1.5
mm.

To compare with the reduced model, the expectation of radial bubble dynamics are obtained by averaging 30 realizations. Two scaling parameters are used to find the application condition of the reduced model. The parameter controlling the effect of the bubble dynamics on the global flow fields is the cloud interaction parameter
B
0
[8], which can be written as:
(19)
B
0
=
Na
0
R
0
.
The nonlinear cloud dynamics are strongly dependent on this parameter [16]. For the bubble-bubble interaction term
3
2
Na
R
0
1 -
1
3
r
j
2
-
D
min
2
R
0
2
2
a
̇
2
+ a
a
¨
, Eq. (19) can be treated as the normalization parameter. Another important parameter is the normalized distance
γ
bb
[3], which can be written as:
(20)
γ
bb
=
d
a
i , max
+
a
j , max
,
where
a
i , max
and
a
j , max
are the radii of the two bubbles at maximum expansion during their oscillation. For staying spherically,
γ
bb
> 10
should be taken for the interaction between bubbles to be considered as weak [3]. However, in strong interaction case, an excellent agreement was found between coupled R-P type equations and experimental data [27] except during the collapse phase where the assumption of sphericity is not valid anymore. Thus, the examination of the reduced model for
γ
bb
< 10
is also important. Using mean bubble spacing
d ≈
4 π /
3 N
3
R
0
, the maximum of
B
0
is approximately equal to
B
0 , max
= 4 π
R
0
2
a
0
/ 3
D
min
3
.

Numerical calculations, illustrated in Fig. 3 , show the comparison between the reduced model and the coupled R-P type model in the weak bubble-bubble interaction regime. The radius-time curves calculated by the K-M equation are also given to show the role which the bubble-bubble interaction plays. The corresponding wall velocities is given in Fig. 4 . Visually, the reduced model can well reproduce the radial motion of the bubbles during the growth stage and roughly reproduce the collapse stage, and the errors of the reduced model in different acoustic cycle are similar to each other. However, the maximum wall velocities (and the local maximum wall velocities during the rebound stages) are always overestimated by the reduced model. Such accumulated errors may induce the inaccurate prediction of the reduced model during the rebound stages. Besides, one of the most important advantage of the reduced model is the reduced computation cost. Our investigation has been performed using the MATLAB (The Math Works Inc., Natick, MA) environment. The computational time for the reduced model is 0.3 s, but the computational time for the coupled R-P type model is 1600 s.

Notice that in some of the simulations the predictions of the K-M model is not accurate due to the large wall velocity. The maximum wall velocity achieved in these simulations are even beyond the limit of the validity of the Gilmore equation [41]. For applying the present model at such intense collapse regimes, the sound speed should be corrected [42–44].

However, with the increasing of the amplitude of the incident wave, the errors in the growth stage and the collapse stage could become larger. To further analysis the ability of the reduced model, two parameters,
a
Error
=
a
( t )
max , coupled
- a
( t )
max , reduced
/ | a
( t )
max , coupled
-
a
0
|
and
t
Error
=
t
col , coupled
-
t
col , reduced
/
T
inc
are used, where
a
( t )
max , coupled
is the maximum bubble radius calculated by the coupled R-P type model,
a
( t )
max , reduced
is the maximum bubble radius calculated by the reduced model,
t
col , coupled
is the collapse time calculated by the coupled R-P type model,
t
col , reduced
is the collapse time calculated by the reduced model, and the
T
inc
is the acoustic period of the incident wave. The rebound stage is not discussed since the prediction of the reduced model in such a stage is inaccurate, and only the errors in the first acoustic cycle is given due to the large computational cost of the coupled R-P type model.

The
a
Error
and
t
Error
is given in Fig. 5 , and three different positions are used to test the accuracy of the reduced model: (1) the bubbles located near the center of the bubble cluster
r
0
= 0.3
R
0
; (2) the bubbles located near the middle of the bubble cluster
r
0
= 0.6
R
0
; (3) the bubbles located near the boundary of the bubble cluster
r
0
= 0.9
R
0
. Notice that the maximum bubble radius can be well reproduced by the reduced model in the weak bubble-bubble interaction case (
a
Error
< 1.5 %
for all the simulations), especially, in quasi-linear oscillation regimes (
P
df
= 0.5 bar , 1 bar
), where the
a
Error
could be smaller than
0.1 %
. In nonlinear regime, as the
B
0
or
P
df
become larger, the accuracy of the reduced models degradate. Fig. 5d shows the error of the collapse time, and only the collapse time in nonlinear regime is used since there is no obvious collapse stage in linear regime. For the weak bubble-bubble intraction regime we tested, the difference of the collapse time between two models could be always small than
0.1 %
.

Such a conclusion is very interesting since, for small values of
B
0
, the pressure inside the cluster is inhomogeneous. However, as shown in the calculation of Section 2.4, the homogeneous assumption required by the reduced model is actually statistical homogeneity. In fact, although the pressure inside a realization of the bubble cluster is inhomogeneous for small values of
B
0
, statistical homogeneity can still be satisfied by the average of enough realizations as long as the bubble distribution is spatially homogeneous.

In addition, it is also interesting to note that the growth phase predicted by coupled R-P type equations are in good agreement with experimental data [27]. Therefore, it is also important to see the difference of the growth phase calculated by the reduced models and the couple R-P type model. Fig. 6 shows the comparison of the maximum bubble radius between two models when
γ
bb
≈ 3
. As the
B
0
becomes larger so does the effect of bubble-bubble interaction on the single bubble dynamics in the cluster, and the growth phase cannot be accurately described by the reduced model when cloud interaction parameter or the amplitude of driving field is large.

In this work, a modified formula of homogenization-based model is presented, the comparison between two frequently used models is given and an inherent distinction in bubble-bubble interaction term between models is found. To gain insight into the mechanisms of such distinction, a reduced model unifying these two models is presented, and such a distinction is explained by the spatial dependence of the bubble-bubble interaction in a bubble cluster accordingly. Such a reduced model, which is simple enough to be numerically tractable, but properly accounts for the evolution of bubbles in a cluster, avoids the challenges in direct numerical simulations, Eulerian-Lagrangian models and the coupled R-P type model. We notice that, (1) for weak bubble-bubble interaction regime (
γ
bb
> 10
), this scheme can be used to predict the growth stage and collapse stage of both linear bubble oscillations and weak nonlinear bubble oscillations; (2) for strong bubble-bubble interaction (
γ
bb
< 10
), this scheme is suitable to calculate the growth stage of bubble oscillations when
B
0
or the amplitude of the external wave is small. The weakness of this model is that the prediction for the rebound stage is always inaccurate.

In addition, we emphasize that the unifying reduced model in this work was proposed under three main restrictions: (1) the unifying reduced model is a statistical model (so does the homogenization-based model and simultaneous motion model). Although the pressure inside the bubble cluster do not have to be uniform, the distribution of bubbles should be approximately spatially homogeneous; (2) this scheme is suitable to the regime where the cloud size is much smaller than the external ultrasound wavelength; (3) the bubble translation and the secondary Bjerknes force are ignored in this model.

For applications, the reduced models we studied have been used as a part of many models including bubble-bubble interaction, such as the bubble two-phase flow model studying unsteady cavitation on a hydrofoil section [45,46], cavitating nozzle flows [47], and so on. Although the reduced models can not calculate the rebound stage accurately, such models work well in simulating the flow in bubbly liquids. Besides, for the single bubble dynamics inside bubble clouds, the reduced model could also be used to study the physical phenomenon like the prediction of multibubble sonoluminescence [39], multi-bubble dynamics for lithotripter where bubbles will be destructed before rebound stage [34,27], and synchronization phenomenon [33], since the rebound stage is not important in these cases. Another possible application of the present model is estimation of natural frequency of medical microbubbles under bubble-bubble interaction and their destruction threshold [48].

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Project No. 41576102, and No. U1709203 were supported by the NSFC. Project No. 3072019GIP0506 was supported by Ph.D. Student Research and Innovation Fund of the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities.
